subject: ap-world-history
title: Short Answer Question — AP World History
stimulus:
“FACTORIES” (TRADING POSTS A ND W AREHOUSES) IN T HE
CITY OF CANTON, SOUTHERN CHINA, 1856
The plan was drawn from a survey of the area carried out by the British Royal Navy
Source: H. B. Morse, The International Relations of the Chinese Empire, (New Y ork: Paragon Book Gallery, 1910 ed.), p. 70.

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE economic d evelopment in the period 1750–1900 that led to the situation represented in the
map.

scoring_points.description: Identify ONE economic development in the period 1750–1900 that led to the situation represented in the map (Canton foreign trading factories).

required_elements: Specific 1750–1900 economic development that explains why European traders were established at Canton (e.g., European industrialization, Chinese economic stagnation, European demand for luxury goods).
alternatives.correct_example: Industrialization in Europe led to more markets in Asia.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Citing a political or military development instead of an economic one.
Citing evidence outside 1750–1900 (e.g., Ming treasure voyages).
common_traps: Naming the Opium Wars (political/military development — better fits part B).
Generic claim that 'Europeans wanted Chinese goods' without identifying the driver.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE economic development in the period 1750–1900 that led to the situation represented in the map. Examples that earn this point include: industrialization in Europe led to more markets in Asia; the stagnation of economic or technological development in China allowed Europeans to pressure their way into the Chinese economy; European desire for luxury goods led to economic imperialism.

sample_responses.response: Industrialization in Europe led to more markets in Asia.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — specific economic development (European industrialization) that drove European commercial presence in Asian ports.

sample_responses.response: The stagnation of economic or technological development in China allowed Europeans to pressure their way into the Chinese economy.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — specific economic development (relative Chinese stagnation) explaining the asymmetry visible in the Canton factories map.

sample_responses.response: European desire for luxury goods led to economic imperialism.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — demand for tea, silk, porcelain, and other luxury goods drove the foreign trading presence at Canton.

parts.prompt: Identify ONE political development in the period 1750–1900 that led to the situation represented in the map.

scoring_points.description: Identify ONE political development in the period 1750–1900 that led to the situation represented in the map (Canton foreign trading factories).

required_elements: Specific 1750–1900 political development that explains the presence of foreign trading factories at Canton (Opium Wars, European colonial expansion in Asia, Taiping Rebellion weakening the Qing, etc.).
alternatives.correct_example: The Opium Wars led to the presence of European merchants in China.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Citing an economic development (should be reserved for part A).
common_traps: Naming 'imperialism' generically without identifying a specific political event or policy.
Repeating the economic development answer from part A.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE political development in the period 1750–1900 that led to the situation represented in the map. Examples that earn this point include: the Opium Wars led to the presence of European merchants in China; European colonial expansion in Asia contributed to the kinds of settlements shown on the map; the Taiping Rebellion weakened China politically during the nineteenth century.

sample_responses.response: The Opium Wars led to the presence of European merchants in China.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — the Opium Wars (1839–42, 1856–60) and resulting unequal treaties directly produced the European factory presence shown in the 1856 map.

sample_responses.response: European colonial expansion in Asia contributed to the kinds of settlements shown on the map.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — broader European colonial expansion across Asia produced the fortified/segregated trading enclaves visible on the map.

sample_responses.response: The Taiping Rebellion weakened China politically during the nineteenth century.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — the Taiping Rebellion (1850–64) weakened Qing authority, enabling expanded European control at Canton.

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE reason why the survey of the Canton factories was carried out by the British Royal Navy.
3
Answer all parts of the question that follows.

scoring_points.description: Explain ONE reason why the survey of the Canton factories was carried out by the British Royal Navy.

required_elements: Specific British strategic, commercial, or competitive reason for the Royal Navy's survey (treaty control of Canton, securing maritime trade, monitoring other European powers).
alternatives.correct_example: The British had gained control of Canton because of treaties with China.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Only identifying the survey without explaining WHY the British conducted it.
Citing unrelated reasons (e.g., Britain's relationship with India).
common_traps: Describing what the map shows rather than explaining the purpose of the survey.
Assuming the Royal Navy was exclusively a scientific agency.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining ONE reason why the survey of the Canton factories was carried out by the British Royal Navy. Examples that earn this point include: the British had gained control of Canton because of treaties with China; the British wanted to expand their revenues from the port of Canton; the British wanted to secure their control over maritime trade entering China; the British were competitive with other European powers and wanted to study their competition.

sample_responses.response: The British had gained control of Canton because of treaties with China.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — treaty-based British control of Canton (e.g., Treaty of Nanjing) gave the Royal Navy the authority and motive to survey the port.

sample_responses.response: The British wanted to secure their control over maritime trade entering China.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — securing maritime commerce into China is a specific strategic reason tied directly to the Royal Navy's role.

sample_responses.response: The British were competitive with other European powers and wanted to study their competition.
sample_responses.commentary: CB-listed example — inter-European imperial competition explains why the Navy would catalog all foreign factories (French, Dutch, American) in the port.
